levers.review: This lever governs the tension between speed and institutional permanence — but the options overlook the fact that eIDAS2 implementing regulations are already largely decided, and NSIS working groups may have incumbent vendor representation that blocks substantive platform-neutrality language. None of the options address how to navigate the risk that EU-level engagement simply delays national action while generating nonbinding guidance that procurement actors can ignore.

levers.name: Institutional Handoff and Long-Term Advocacy Ownership
levers.consequences: By month 24, the project must transition feasibility evidence, demonstrators, and policy momentum to actors who can sustain advocacy beyond the project's formal end. This requires clarity on whether Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, a civil-society organization, an academic consortium, or a coalition of private-sector actors takes ownership of platform-neutrality advocacy. A government handoff provides authority but risks bureaucratic absorption and loss of independence; civil-society ownership preserves advocacy momentum but lacks procurement authority; academic ownership offers credibility but limited operational leverage. The choice also affects which stakeholders feel responsibility for activating fallback paths operationally if they are formally adopted.
options: Structure the project to explicitly hand evidence and advocacy leadership to Datatilsynet or another existing Danish regulatory body with procurement oversight, accepting that independent pressure transforms into internal governance process.

Establish an independent coalition entity (a formal network or working group) co-hosted by civil-society and academic partners with funding commitments beyond the project, positioned to sustain platform-neutrality advocacy indefinitely and serve as a continuity-planning reference post-AltID implementation.

Design the project's final deliverables as a published, independently referenced evidence base and standards proposal that becomes a public good, allowing distributed stakeholder ownership and reducing dependence on a single institutional actor or ongoing coalition funding.
levers.review: This lever frames the tension between institutional leverage and advocacy independence — but the options do not address the likelihood that formal handoff to government bodies will result in the work being deprioritized once the immediate AltID procurement window closes. None of the options account for how to ensure fallback paths, once formally adopted, are actually maintained and exercised operationally rather than becoming dormant regulatory requirements.

levers.name: Risk Taxonomy and Institutional Framing
levers.consequences: How the project articulates the core problem determines which stakeholders listen and what language appears in policy documents. Framing the issue as continuity-of-service risk, supplier concentration, and resilience-planning weakness activates procurement and governance bodies. By contrast, framing it as privacy activism or anti-monopoly ideology triggers defensive responses from platform vendors and incumbent operators. The risk is that oversimplifying the frame loses credibility with technical experts, or that reframing too heavily toward risk displaces the genuine digital-sovereignty concern.
options: Commission an external audit of Danish digital identity's supplier concentration and contingency-planning gaps, positioning the output as a civil-continuity and procurement-resilience document rather than a platform-criticism manifesto.

Anchor the framing in Denmark's published 2026–2029 digital strategy goals on supplier independence and sovereignty, citing the strategy directly in all policy submissions to establish institutional alignment rather than external pressure.

Develop a scenario-planning document for Digitaliseringsstyrelsen modeling authentication disruption under geopolitical or supply-chain shock, with fallback-access provisions positioned as standard governance and business-continuity practice.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between technical legitimacy and political acceptability, but the options underestimate how incumbent operators will reframe continuity arguments as FUD (fear, uncertainty, doubt). None of the options address the counternarrative: how to respond when Apple or Google claim their platforms already provide resilience and that alternatives introduce unproven security trade-offs.

levers.name: Technical Demonstrator Positioning and Assurance Depth
levers.consequences: A demonstrator that authenticates against a simulated identity provider proves feasibility but lacks credibility if reviewers question its security assumptions or claim it oversimplifies real MitID constraints. Investing in external security review, formal documentation, and published security analysis increases institutional trust but consumes time and budget. A lightweight demonstrator shipped quickly may generate early visibility but risks being dismissed as a toy; a rigorous demonstrator takes longer and may miss critical policy windows.
options: Commission a formal security assessment of the mobile demonstrator from an independent external firm recognized by Danish financial-sector regulators, publishing the full assessment to establish third-party credibility.

Structure the demonstrator as an explicitly experimental artifact with published assumptions, limitations, and threat model, positioning it as a reference implementation rather than a production prototype to manage expectations and defend scope boundaries.

Implement the demonstrator using only Danish-aligned or EU-approved components and standards (e.g., eIDAS-certified libraries, NSIS-compatible protocols), documenting compliance to reduce the surface for incumbent objections based on vendor lock-in concerns.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between speed and institutional credibility, but the options do not address the core trap: a demonstrator's credibility depends partly on technical rigor and partly on political legitimacy, and these two dimensions can conflict. A heavily audited demonstrator may take months to complete, by which time AltID's design window may have narrowed; a fast demonstrator risks being used against the project if it contains flaws.

levers.name: Coalition Composition and Accountability Anchor
levers.consequences: Building a coalition of privacy advocates, academics, and civil-society groups creates political pressure and institutional cover, but coalition members have different success criteria and risk tolerance. Privacy advocates may push toward stronger data-minimization messaging; security experts may emphasize cryptographic assurance; contingency planners may focus on service continuity. If the coalition lacks a shared accountability anchor—a formal institutional partner with skin in the game—the coalition becomes a lobbying coalition rather than a stakeholder coalition, reducing weight in procurement conversations.
options: Secure a formal commitment from a Danish financial-sector institution or critical-infrastructure operator to co-author a policy proposal or public statement linking AltID requirements to their own continuity-planning obligations.

Recruit members of Folketinget's digital-policy committee to serve as formal sponsors of a parliamentary inquiry into supplier concentration in Danish digital identity, leveraging legislative momentum to structure coalition work.

Establish a formal technical working group convened by a neutral institution (e.g., Danish Standards Organisation or an academic research council) with rotating membership and published meeting minutes, creating institutional permanence beyond the project's 24-month timeline.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between coalition breadth and accountability clarity, but the options overlook the free-rider problem: how to ensure coalition members remain invested in the project's success and do not extract credit or fade when institutional pressure intensifies. None of the options address exit strategies if key coalition members withdraw or if coalition unity fractures under direct opposition from incumbent operators.

levers.name: Procurement Language and Standards Lock-In Point
levers.consequences: Procurement language inserted into AltID's requirements documents, tenders, or technical specifications becomes durable once embedded in formal evaluation criteria and contractual terms. Earlier insertion (e.g., during design-phase consultations) is easier but may not survive political objections from incumbent operators; later insertion (e.g., during final tender drafting) is harder to achieve but may have higher probability of surviving to implementation. Timing too late risks that the architecture is already locked in; timing too early risks that language is vague or overruled by procurement amendments.
options: Identify the specific milestone in AltID's project timeline where procurement requirements are finalized, and target submission of detailed technical language to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen at least six weeks before that milestone, backed by a published feasibility report and formal institutional co-sponsorship.

Propose that platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements be included in AltID's supplier evaluation criteria rather than in mandatory technical specifications, allowing flexibility in implementation while making supplier concentration a scored differentiator.

Engage with the EU's eIDAS2 regulatory working groups to publish guidance on platform-neutral wallet design and fallback authentication, then cite EU guidance in Danish AltID consultations to invoke supranational standards pressure.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between timing certainty and insertion depth, but the options do not address the scenario where Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or the AltID operator faces direct political or commercial pressure to exclude platform-neutrality language. None of the options include contingency messaging or alternative policy levers if the primary procurement insertion fails.

levers.name: Demonstrator Platform Selection and Ecosystem Signal
levers.consequences: Choosing GrapheneOS or a comparable privacy-respecting Android variant as the demonstrator platform signals commitment to open alternatives but may undermine claims of mainstream accessibility, since GrapheneOS adoption remains low. Alternatively, using standard Android demonstrates feasibility on widely used infrastructure but invites arguments that the platform is already dominated by Google, so the demonstrator does not prove vendor independence. The chosen platform implicitly endorses an architectural path and telegraphs which ecosystem the project assumes as the fallback.
options: Implement the mobile demonstrator on stock Android using only open-source or vendor-neutral dependencies, partnering with the Android Security & Privacy Research community to publish the work as an open ecosystem reference rather than a GrapheneOS-exclusive design.

Deploy the demonstrator on GrapheneOS alongside a parallel analysis document explaining why privacy-respecting Android distributions are necessary trust anchors for platform-neutral digital identity, positioning the choice as a technical requirement rather than an ideological preference.

Build the demonstrator to be agnostic to the underlying Android distribution, using containerization and published interfaces so that future deployers can run it on GrapheneOS, standard Android, or other compatible platforms without code changes.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between ecosystem pragmatism and digital sovereignty commitment, but the options underestimate how platform choice becomes a political signal. Choosing GrapheneOS signals radical platform independence but may invite dismissal as niche; choosing stock Android risks being perceived as collaborating with Google's ecosystem. None of the options address how to navigate accusations that the chosen platform itself depends on foreign vendors.

levers.name: EU Standards Alignment and De-Coupling from Danish Procurement Hostility
levers.consequences: Engaging with European Digital Identity Wallet forums and eIDAS2 working groups creates a higher-level policy lever that can bypass or frame Danish procurement resistance. If European guidance language supports platform neutrality and fallback access, Danish authorities face pressure to comply. However, EU-level engagement consumes time and political capital; if the European working groups are themselves captured by incumbent vendors or move slowly, the investment may not yield actionable leverage. The project risks becoming a European-advocacy effort rather than a Danish intervention.
options: Position the project's feasibility report and demonstrators as contributions to the EU's European Digital Identity Wallet reference architecture discussions, submitting formally to the relevant technical working group and citing the work in all Danish procurement submissions.

Coordinate with sympathetic EU member states (e.g., those with published digital-sovereignty strategies or open-standards mandates) to co-author a joint position paper on platform-neutral wallet design, presented to the eIDAS2 coordination group.

Track the European Digital Identity Wallet's procurement tender timeline and ensure the project's research outputs are published and available for citation before the EU's tender documentation is finalized, positioning Danish work as evidence for EU-wide requirements.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between scope expansion and leverage amplification, but the options assume Danish priorities align with broader EU agendas. If the EU's wallet work is also influenced by platform vendors or if EU guidance becomes vague or non-binding, the project's EU engagement may consume resources without translating into Danish policy pressure.

levers.name: Exit Criteria and Graceful Descoping if Institutional Traction Fails
levers.consequences: The project's success depends partly on institutional buy-in from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or AltID operators, which the project cannot fully control. If institutional traction is weak by month 16, the project faces a choice: continue pursuing procurement influence (likely futile and budget-consuming) or pivot to publication, coalition consolidation, and standards contribution (lower-impact but achievable). Without clear exit criteria, the project risks burning remaining budget on false hope; with explicit exit criteria, the project can shift messaging and maintain credibility. The risk is that pivoting to 'soft' outcomes (publication, standards input) appears as failure to external stakeholders.
options: Establish measurable gate criteria at month 16: if no formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has been received and no AltID-related procurement language has been proposed, trigger a pivot to consolidation mode, with phase three focusing on coalition permanence, standards work, and published research rather than procurement intervention.

Develop two parallel narrative tracks from the beginning: one focused on procurement and institutional change, one focused on long-term European standards and ecosystem development. If institutional leverage fails, shift communication emphasis to the standards track without claiming a change in strategy.

Secure explicit agreement from the project's advisory board or financing institution that successful completion of phase three includes publication of research, coalition maintenance, and EU-level standards contribution as legitimate outcomes even if formal AltID procurement influence is not achieved.
levers.review: This lever governs the tension between ambition and realism, but the options do not address the political cost of perceived failure. If the project shifts to 'softer' outcomes after months of messaging about procurement change, stakeholders and funders may interpret the shift as a retreat, undermining coalition confidence and future institutional leverage. None of the options provide language or framing to communicate descoping as a strategic adjustment rather than a setback.

Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.

5. **Prohibitions**

   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**

Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.
